findings.suggestion: Use a parameterized query: cursor = db.execute("SELECT id, name FROM products WHERE name LIKE ?", (f"%{name}%",)); return cursor.fetchall(). Never interpolate caller-supplied values into SQL text.
findings.summary: Line 8 builds the query with f"SELECT id, name FROM products WHERE name LIKE '%{name}%'" and passes it straight to db.execute(). The untrusted `name` parameter is interpolated into the SQL text with no binding or escaping, so a value such as "%' UNION SELECT id, password FROM users --" injects arbitrary SQL. The comment on lines 6-7 documents this exact exploit but the shipped code does not remediate it — stated intent and implementation both confirm the vulnerable pattern is what was committed.
findings.title: SQL injection via f-string interpolation in search_products

nodes.label: Prior commits, decisions, agents, and command evidence

nodes.detail: blocked · No candidate is marked confirmed or fixed using stale or missing verification.
nodes.label: rerun_relevant_verification

summary:
Risk-tiered review (trivial) completed.

Single new file adding search_products, which contains a textbook SQL injection: the `name` argument is interpolated into the SQL string via f-string on line 8 and executed without parameterization. The vulnerability is provable by static reading and is even self-documented in the comment (lines 6-7). Currently 0 callers, so it is a latent sink rather than an actively exploitable path, but it is critical the moment it is wired into any request handler. Fix by switching to bound parameters.

Single new function with a textbook SQL injection: user-controlled `name` is f-string-interpolated into the SQL statement on line 8. Statically provable (no runtime state dependency), so reported at high confidence despite the blocked verification gate. Currently has 0 callers, so it is a latent hole rather than an actively exploited path, but it must be fixed before anything wires request input into it.
